danger_signals.visual_form: Ball sprite moving toward player team
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat; player must catch or dodge

danger_signals.visual_form: Character transforms into angel caricature and flies upward off-screen
danger_signals.meaning: Loss of team strength; reduced life bar

danger_signals.visual_form: Captain character winds up for specialized throw with faster ball travel
danger_signals.meaning: High-damage attack; multiple hits required to eliminate opponent

danger_signals.visual_form: Fewer characters on player's side of court than opponent
danger_signals.meaning: Team is losing; risk of match failure if time expires

safety_signals.visual_form: Ball sprite held by player character
safety_signals.meaning: Player can attack or pass; control of match tempo

safety_signals.visual_form: Player character animation catches incoming ball
safety_signals.meaning: Threat neutralized; possession transferred to player team

safety_signals.visual_form: All seven team members visible on court (captain + 6 members)
safety_signals.meaning: Team at full strength; optimal defensive and offensive capability

safety_signals.visual_form: Timer display; match progresses without elimination
safety_signals.meaning: If team has more survivors when time expires, player wins regardless of ball possession

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: National flag (US version displays flag; Japanese version uses school uniform)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Dallas vs. Chicago team roster differs in character appearance (skin tone, hair color reflect Americanization)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Court background changes per match; USA home court music and visual setting

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: National flag (US version); school insignia (Japanese version as Hanazono High School)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Distinct home court background from Dallas

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Union Jack flag (incorrectly used in US version; source notes confusion with English flag)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Four offensive players plus backup roster
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Stage music references Beatles hits ('Get Back' + 'A Hard Day's Night' mashup); British-themed court

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple backup players not visible on main court
Four main court players with bench reserves

faction_indicators.faction: Japan (Final opponent, or home team in Japanese version)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: National flag (US version); Nekketsu High School insignia (Japanese version)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: In Japanese version, Nekketsu High School is player team; Hanazono is rival. In US version, roles reversed with team names/stats swapped
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Japan stage theme uses 'Sakura Sakura'; distinct court background. Home court music and visuals swapped between versions

faction_indicators.faction: USSR (Soviet team, US version final opponent)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Derived from original Team USA data; stats and visual presentation reflect Cold War-era finalization
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Court and music associated with original Team USA; final match significance in US version reflects late 1980s geopolitics

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Match order: Dallas vs. Chicago → England → Iceland → China → Kenya/Africa → Japan (US version); England → India → Iceland → China → Kenya → Japan → USA (Japanese version order before regional swap)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Difficulty scaling: early opponents have fewer backup players and simpler AI; player team adjusts roster composition

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Tournament climax / Cold War era context (US version)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Final match against USSR team (geopolitical substitution); court and music are swapped from original Team USA presentation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Final opponent uses most aggressive tactics; all backup roster members employed; highest stats on opposing team

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Timer visible on-screen; no eliminations occurring
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Winning condition shifts: team with most surviving members wins if timer expires; strategic camping/defense becomes viable

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Entire opposing team transformed into angel caricatures; all characters fly off-screen; trophy awarded by ninja character (Superman parody in arcade version)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Tournament advances to next opponent; match setup resets with new team; world cycle restarts after defeating final team

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Japanese version: domestic school rivalry (Nekketsu vs. Hanazono) begins tournament; international teams follow. US version: national team selection (Team USA vs. All-Stars) precedes world cup; USSR positioned as final boss
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Team roster swapping: Team Japan receives Team USA's stats/formation; Team USSR receives Team USA's original stats; character faces remain unchanged, creating cognitive dissonance in localization

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Player team customization enabled (Japanese version only)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Name entry screen displays custom team and player names; team identity displayed in World Cup, Versus Play, and Bean Ball modes
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: US version disabled this feature entirely; pressing Select produces sound effect but no action

environmental_storytelling.example: England stage features mashup of Beatles songs ('Get Back' + 'A Hard Day's Night'); Japan stage uses traditional 'Sakura Sakura'; each nation's court background reflects cultural/geographical identity

environmental_storytelling.technique: Backup roster system as world-state indicator
environmental_storytelling.example: Foreign teams have more than four offensive players available; when a member is eliminated, backup players replace them, signaling deep bench depth and tournament-level competition
US version changed character skin tones and hair colors from Japanese version to reflect Americanization; visual appearance communicates team affiliation and geopolitical context

environmental_storytelling.technique: Captain design as faction power indicator
environmental_storytelling.example: Each team's captain is larger-sized character with exclusive 'Power Shot' ability; captain prominence signals leadership structure and specialized threat level

environmental_storytelling.technique: Flag display as political/national identity marker
environmental_storytelling.example: US version displays national flags for each team on selection screen; Japanese version omits flags, using school uniforms instead; localization choice reflects regional audience expectations

environmental_storytelling.technique: Final boss visual elevation via trophy ceremony
environmental_storytelling.example: Victory animatic features ninja (or Superman parody in arcade) awarding trophy; visual celebration ritual reinforces tournament hierarchy and geopolitical dominance

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Can execute 'Power Shot' (faster travel, higher damage) by dashing twice and throwing; visual wind-up animation distinguishes from regular throw
Employs Power Shot with increased frequency as match progresses; targets player team captain preferentially
Character remains on court but shows visual damage indicator (reduced animation frames or staggered movement); one final hit triggers elimination
Transforms into angel caricature and floats upward off-screen; creates darkly comedic tone while signaling loss condition
Replaces fallen teammate on court; visually appears from sideline area; maintains team offensive/defensive balance

entity_behavioral_tells.state: Opponent has ball, incoming throw detected
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Right button triggers duck/dodge animation; left button triggers catch animation; successful catch transfers possession; failed dodge applies damage
Increases throwing frequency and Power Shot usage; targets player team captain to eliminate captain advantage

notes: Super Dodge Ball demonstrates aggressive localization strategy: US version performs complete team name/data swapping to reposition USSR as final antagonist (Cold War context, late 1980s), while Japanese version features domestic school rivalry (Nekketsu High School vs. Hanazono High School). Visual identity systems rely heavily on national flags, character appearance alterations, and stage-specific audio/background art. Faction differentiation achieved through roster depth (backup player system), captain design prominence, and uniform color coding. World-state communication minimal; progression is linear tournament structure without dynamic world changes. Regional differences include disabled name-entry feature in US version, disabled microphone easter eggs (NES lacks hardware), and stage order reordering. Visual danger/safety signals depend entirely on ball possession, team roster status, and timer pressure. No complex environmental state shifts; game maintains consistent court setup throughout.
